How Putin Turned Japan Into a Den of Spies
Earlier this year, I found myself on the 22nd floor of a Tokyo high rise. I was looking for a man named Maksim Vladimirovich Filchenkov, a Russian spy.
Filchenkov, officials told us, is part of the 20th Directorate of Russia’s military intelligence agency. The 20th works to smuggle high-tech equipment into Russia to support the war in Ukraine.
Japan is key to that effort. Dozens of Russian spies have turned up there in recent years. An estimated 90 percent of Russian missiles and drones contain Japanese components.
Filchenkov works out of the Tokyo office of Aeroflot, the Russian state-owned airline that has long been used as cover for Russian spies. I set out to track him down.
